items.mistake: Setting only one random seed while using multiple libraries
items.fix: Seed Python random, NumPy, PyTorch/TensorFlow, and pass random_state to sklearn splits — in one init block before any random operation.
items.why: Each library maintains separate RNG state; seeding only NumPy leaves PyTorch weight init and DataLoader shuffles uncontrolled.
items.example: Wrong: only np.random.seed(0). Right: also random.seed(0), torch.manual_seed(0), and train_test_split(..., random_state=0).

items.mistake: Changing the data split seed between hyperparameter trials
items.fix: Fix random_state on train/val/test and CV folds for the entire sweep; change only the hyperparameter under test.
items.why: Different splits confound hyperparameter quality with partition luck — a bad split can make a good learning rate look worse.
items.example: Sweep lr in {1e-2, 1e-3} with random_state=42 for both runs, not 42 vs 99.

items.mistake: Logging metrics without seed or environment metadata
items.fix: Record seed(s), git commit, requirements lock file or Docker tag, and Python/CUDA versions in every experiment run.
items.why: Metrics without context cannot be reproduced or defended in review — see Tracking Metrics for logging patterns.

items.mistake: Assuming GPU training is deterministic by default
items.fix: Enable framework deterministic flags where available, document hardware, and accept small numeric drift; compare with tolerance not exact equality.
items.why: Some CUDA reductions and atomic ops are nondeterministic unless explicitly configured, often at a speed penalty.
items.example: torch.use_deterministic_algorithms(True) may error on unsupported ops — log which flags were used.
